worth_it_explanation: Ad pod settings decide how a Connected TV commercial break is structured, how long it runs, and how many ads fill it, so they shape both the revenue a break can earn and how a viewer experiences it. Pod settings left at their defaults can run breaks that are too long, too repetitive, or stacked with competing brands, which trades viewer trust and completion rates for short-term fill. The check is a review of the pod configuration a publisher already holds in Index Exchange and its header-bidding wrapper, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete pod setting the publisher can adjust.
intro: An ad pod is a Connected TV (CTV) commercial break that plays one or more video ads back to back inside a stream, the way a television viewer sees a break between segments. On a sell-side platform (SSP) such as Index Exchange, the pod's structure is carried by the bid request the publisher sends, so the duration, the number of ads, and the separation rules a publisher sets decide what buyers can fill and what a viewer ends up watching. This subcategory is about whether those pod settings are deliberate choices rather than wrapper defaults, and whether competitive separation and frequency rules inside the pod protect the viewing experience. The structure rides on OpenRTB pod-bidding signals and Prebid ad pod configuration, so the audit looks at the values a publisher chose and how their header-bidding setup expresses them to Index Exchange.

audit_checks.title: Ad pod duration and slot structure run on wrapper defaults
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Pod duration and the number of ads per pod set how long a viewer waits and how the break clears. A pod left at default values can run longer than the content warrants or stack more ads than the audience tolerates, which raises drop-off and lowers completion rates, so a break filled on autopilot can erode the same inventory value it was meant to capture.

educate.base:
A Connected TV (CTV) ad pod is the commercial break a viewer sees between segments of a stream, and its shape is set by a handful of values: how long the whole pod runs, how long each ad inside it may run, and how many ad slots the break holds. On Index Exchange, those values travel in the bid request the publisher sends, so the structure a buyer fills is the structure the publisher defined upstream in its header-bidding wrapper. When the wrapper carries default values rather than ones the publisher chose, the break that reaches the viewer is an accident of configuration rather than a decision.

The pod structure is expressed through OpenRTB pod-bidding signals and, in a Prebid-based wrapper, through ad pod settings the publisher configures. The total pod duration tells buyers how much commercial time the break holds. The allowed ad durations tell them which creative lengths fit, so a buyer can return, for example, two 30-second ads, four 15-second ads, or a mix that fills the same time. Whether the returned creatives must match an exact duration or may be rounded to the nearest allowed length is a separate setting that changes how tightly the pod fills. Each of these is a lever the publisher controls, and each affects both revenue and the viewing experience.

The cost of accepting defaults shows up on both sides of the trade. A pod set longer than the content warrants gives buyers more slots to fill, which can raise the break's revenue, but a viewer facing a long stack of ads is more likely to drop off before the content resumes, which lowers completion and the value of the next break. A pod set with loose duration rules can fill with mismatched creative lengths that leave silent gaps or truncated ads. Neither outcome is visible in a fill-rate number alone, so a publisher reading only fill can miss a pod structure that is quietly working against retention.

A few signs point to pod settings running on defaults:
  • The total pod duration and maximum number of ads per pod match the wrapper's out-of-box values rather than a number the publisher chose for the content
  • No record exists of why the current pod duration and ad count were set, beyond them being the suggested configuration
  • Allowed ad durations and the exact-duration rule were never reviewed against the creative lengths buyers actually return
You may want to confirm that the pod duration, allowed ad durations, and ads-per-pod count were set deliberately for the content and audience rather than inherited from the wrapper. One approach is to read completion rate and revenue per pod against the current structure, then test a shorter or differently structured pod on a slice of inventory to see how the trade moves.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media publisher's CTV inventory, the natural break length depends on the content, so a mid-roll inside a short news clip can tolerate far less commercial time than a break inside a long-form show. You may want to set pod duration and ad count per content type rather than applying one pod structure across the catalog, so short content carries a shorter break and long content can hold a fuller one without pushing viewers to drop off.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content publisher's video inventory, the audience is often watching product or how-to content with a shopping intent, and a long ad stack between them and the content can send them away from the page entirely. You may want to keep pod duration tighter on commercial video so the break does not outweigh the content the viewer came for, then read completion and on-site behavior to confirm the structure holds the audience.

educate.learn_more_note: Index Exchange's detailed configuration documentation for CTV pod settings is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the pod duration, allowed ad durations, and maximum ads per pod in the header-bidding wrapper, and compare them against the wrapper's default values to see whether they were chosen or inherited.

wrapper_config.label: Header-bidding wrapper configuration
steps: Open the CTV or long-form video ad unit configuration in the Prebid-based wrapper that feeds Index Exchange.
Record the total pod duration setting, the allowed ad durations, and the maximum number of ads per pod.
Note whether the returned creatives must match an exact duration or may be rounded to an allowed length.
Compare each value against the wrapper's documented default to see whether the publisher set it deliberately or left the default in place.

bid_request_inspection.label: OpenRTB bid request inspection
steps: Capture a sample CTV bid request the wrapper sends to Index Exchange.
Locate the pod-bidding fields that carry total pod duration and the pod structure, for example the pod duration and sequence signals.
Confirm the values in the request match the intended pod structure rather than a placeholder or default carried through unchanged.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the pod duration, allowed ad durations, and maximum ads per pod, alongside whether each was set deliberately or matches the wrapper default. A pod structure chosen for the content and audience, with the values and the reasoning on record, passes. A pod structure that matches the wrapper defaults with no stated reason for the duration or ad count is a fail recorded with the settings and the content they apply to.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the pod duration, allowed ad durations, and ads-per-pod count, and whether each was chosen or left at default, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs its Connected TV (CTV) ad pods on the header-bidding wrapper's default duration and slot structure rather than values chosen for the content. Pod duration and the number of ads per pod set how long a viewer waits and how the break clears, so a pod left at default values can run longer or hold more ads than the audience tolerates, which raises drop-off and lowers completion even when fill rate looks healthy. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the pod duration, allowed ad durations, and ads-per-pod count against the content they apply to, setting deliberate values per content type, and reading completion rate and revenue per pod to confirm the structure holds the audience. After the change, recheck completion against the new pod structure to see whether the trade between fill and retention improved.

audit_checks.title: Competitive separation and frequency within the pod are not enforced
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without competitive separation and frequency rules inside a pod, a single break can show two competing brands back to back or repeat the same ad twice. That breaks the television-like experience viewers expect from Connected TV, gives advertisers a reason to pull spend over brand-adjacency concerns, and lowers completion, so a pod that fills without these rules can cost both demand and audience trust.

educate.base:
A Connected TV (CTV) viewer expects a break to behave like television, where competing brands do not run back to back and the same ad does not play twice in one break. Inside an ad pod, holding to that expectation takes deliberate configuration since a pod assembled from programmatic demand can otherwise place two car brands or two quick-service restaurants in adjacent slots, or return the same creative more than once. Competitive separation is the rule that keeps ads from the same product category out of the same pod, and frequency capping within the pod is the rule that keeps a single creative from repeating inside one break.

These controls work through signals that travel with the pod. Pod deduplication, defined in the OpenRTB pod-bidding work, gives the sell side a way to tell buyers which categories or creatives are already present in a pod so they do not return a conflicting ad. A universal creative identifier carried in the VAST response lets the system recognize the same ad across bidders, which is what makes within-pod frequency capping reliable rather than a guess based on creative metadata. On Index Exchange and its header-bidding wrapper, the publisher's job is to make sure these separation and frequency signals are configured and passed, so the buyers filling the pod have what they need to honor them.

The cost of skipping these rules is paid in two currencies. Advertisers watch brand adjacency closely on CTV, and a brand that finds its ad next to a direct competitor, or repeated in the same break, has a concrete reason to pull spend or demand a discount, which thins the demand a publisher can draw. Viewers, meanwhile, read a repetitive or competitor-stacked break as a sign of a low-quality experience, which lowers completion and the value of the inventory that follows. Neither cost shows up in fill rate, so a pod that fills cleanly can still be carrying both.

A few signs point to separation and frequency rules being absent inside the pod:
  • The wrapper passes no competitive-category or pod-deduplication signal, so buyers cannot tell what is already in the pod
  • No within-pod frequency rule exists, so the same creative can appear more than once in a single break
  • Spot checks of recorded pods show competing brands in adjacent slots or a repeated ad inside one pod
You may want to confirm that competitive separation and within-pod frequency rules are configured and that the signals carrying them reach Index Exchange. One approach is to review the pod-deduplication and creative-identifier configuration in the wrapper, then audit a sample of recorded pods for category clashes and repeats.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media publisher's CTV inventory, premium content draws brand advertisers who are the most sensitive to adjacency, so a competitor clash inside a pod on flagship content can cost a direct-sold relationship as well as the open-auction value. You may want to hold competitive separation strictest on the premium inventory, where the demand most worth protecting is also the most likely to walk over a brand-safety lapse.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content publisher's video inventory, the advertisers are often retail and product brands whose direct competitors bid on the same audience, which raises the odds of a category clash inside a single pod. You may want to confirm that product-category separation is configured for the verticals that dominate your demand, so two competing retailers do not land in the same break in front of a shopping audience.

educate.learn_more_note: Index Exchange's detailed configuration documentation for pod competitive separation and frequency is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the competitive-separation and within-pod frequency configuration in the header-bidding wrapper, confirm the signals reach Index Exchange, and audit a sample of recorded pods for category clashes and repeats.

wrapper_config.label: Header-bidding wrapper configuration
steps: Open the CTV or long-form video ad unit configuration in the Prebid-based wrapper that feeds Index Exchange.
Check whether a competitive-category exclusion or pod-deduplication setting is enabled, so buyers are told which categories are already in the pod.
Check whether a within-pod frequency rule is set, so a single creative cannot repeat in one break.
Confirm a universal creative identifier is required on incoming pod bids, which is what makes within-pod frequency capping reliable across bidders.

pod_spot_check.label: Recorded pod spot check
steps: Collect a sample of recorded CTV pods that served, capturing the ads in sequence.
Review each pod for two ads in the same product category placed back to back, which signals separation is not enforced.
Review each pod for the same creative appearing more than once, which signals within-pod frequency capping is absent.
Record the rate of category clashes and repeats across the sample to show how often the rules are breaking down.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether competitive separation and within-pod frequency rules are configured, whether the signals carrying them reach Index Exchange, and the rate of category clashes and repeats in a sample of recorded pods. A setup with separation and frequency rules configured, the signals passing, and a clean pod sample passes. A setup missing either rule, or one where the pod sample shows competing brands or repeated creatives, is a fail recorded with the missing control and the clash rate observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether competitive separation and within-pod frequency rules are configured, and the clash and repeat rate from your pod sample, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not enforce competitive separation and within-pod frequency on its Connected TV (CTV) ad pods, so a single break can place competing brands in adjacent slots or repeat the same ad. CTV viewers expect a break to behave like television, and advertisers watch brand adjacency closely, so a pod without these rules can lose both completion and the demand of brands that pull spend over a competitor clash. {{voice_recommend}} configuring competitive-category separation and a within-pod frequency rule in the header-bidding wrapper, confirming the pod-deduplication and creative-identifier signals reach Index Exchange, and auditing a sample of recorded pods for clashes and repeats. After the change, recheck a fresh pod sample to confirm the separation and frequency rules are holding in the breaks that serve.
